questions.q: What is a recurrence relation and what form does it take for divide-and-conquer algorithms?
options: A formula that only applies to recursive functions, with no closed-form solution

A formula expressing the runtime T(n) in terms of T(smaller subproblems); for divide-and-conquer: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a = number of subproblems, n/b = subproblem size, and f(n) = cost of dividing and combining
A recurrence is the same as a loop; there is no difference
Recurrences only apply to sorting algorithms
questions.explanation: A recurrence relation is recursive: T(n) depends on T(smaller inputs). For divide-and-conquer, the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) is canonical. Example: merge sort has a=2 (divide into 2 halves), b=2 (each half is n/2), f(n)=n (merging costs n). Solving requires techniques: substitution, recursion tree, or Master Theorem. Base case: T(1) = O(1). The recursive structure directly mirrors the algorithm's logic: divide problem, solve subproblems, combine results.

questions.q: State the Master Theorem and explain its three cases.
options: The Master Theorem solves all recurrences, including non-polynomial functions

For T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), compare f(n) to n^(log_b a): (1) if f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)) for ε>0, then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)); (2) if f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) log n); (3) if f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)) for ε>0 and af(n/b) ≤ cf(n), then T(n) = Θ(f(n))
The Master Theorem is only useful for academic problems, not practical algorithms
The three cases depend on whether the recurrence is linear, quadratic, or exponential
questions.explanation: The Master Theorem elegantly solves T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) by determining whether combining (f(n)) or solving subproblems (aT(n/b)) dominates. Case 1: subproblems dominate → T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)). Case 2: both contribute equally → T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) log n). Case 3: combining dominates → T(n) = Θ(f(n)). Example: merge sort (a=2, b=2, f(n)=n) falls into case 2 since n = Θ(n^1) → Θ(n log n). Does not apply if f(n) is irregular or the recurrence is non-standard.

questions.q: How does merge sort partition the problem and what is its complexity?
options: It sorts the array by repeatedly dividing it into single elements, then merging pairs in sorted order

Merge sort divides the array in half repeatedly (divide), recursively sorts each half (conquer), then merges the two sorted halves in O(n) time (combine); T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n solves to Θ(n log n) time and Θ(n) auxiliary space, stable and comparison-based
Merge sort is an in-place algorithm with O(1) extra space
Merge sort has worst-case complexity O(n²)
questions.explanation: Merge sort exemplifies divide-and-conquer: divide array into halves until size 1 (depth log n), merge pairs bottom-up. Merging is two-pointer scanning of sorted subarrays, appending the smaller element; O(n) per merge, n comparisons total. Recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n satisfies Master Theorem case 2 → T(n) = Θ(n log n) guaranteed regardless of input. Stability: if elements are equal, left array element is chosen first, preserving relative order. Space: requires O(n) temporary array. Disadvantage: not in-place; advantage: predictable performance.

questions.q: What distinguishes the average-case and worst-case complexity of quicksort, and why does randomization help?
options: Quicksort always has O(n log n) complexity regardless of input

Quicksort achieves O(n log n) average-case by dividing roughly equally (random pivot), but O(n²) worst-case if pivot is always extremal (sorted input, unlucky choice); randomizing the pivot choice makes worst-case O(n²) unlikely (probability exponentially small), providing O(n log n) expected time with high probability
Quicksort is always O(n log n) on random inputs but O(n) on sorted inputs
Randomization in quicksort guarantees O(n log n) worst-case
questions.explanation: Quicksort's complexity hinges on partition quality. Ideal: pivot splits array 1:n-1 or better → depth O(log n), time O(n log n). Worst: pivot always extremal → depth O(n), time O(n²). Deterministic pivot (first, last, median) can be adversarial on specific inputs (e.g., sorted data with first-element pivot). Randomized pivot: choosing uniformly at random makes the worst-case O(n²) with low probability (<2^(-n)). Expected recurrence: E[T(n)] = E[T(Left)] + E[T(Right)] + n → E[T(n)] = O(n log n) on any input. In-place O(log n) space (recursion depth).

questions.q: Explain the Karatsuba algorithm for integer multiplication and how it reduces complexity.
options: Karatsuba replaces base-10 multiplication with base-2, reducing operations

Karatsuba divides two n-digit numbers X and Y into halves: X=a·2^(n/2)+b, Y=c·2^(n/2)+d, then computes ac, bd, and (a+b)(c+d) (3 recursive multiplications instead of 4), reducing complexity from O(n²) schoolbook multiplication to O(n^1.585)
Karatsuba is only faster for very large numbers (over 10⁶ digits)
Karatsuba uses FFT and is the same as fast polynomial multiplication
questions.explanation: Schoolbook multiplication: X·Y = ac·2^n + (ad+bc)·2^(n/2) + bd requires 4 multiplications of (n/2)-digit numbers. Karatsuba computes: P1=ac, P2=bd, P3=(a+b)(c+d)=ac+ad+bc+bd; then ad+bc = P3-P1-P2, giving X·Y = P1·2^n + (P3-P1-P2)·2^(n/2) + P2 with only 3 multiplications. Recurrence: T(n) = 3T(n/2) + O(n) → T(n) = O(n^(log_2 3)) = O(n^1.585). Faster than O(n²) for large n. Practical for cryptography (thousands of digits); FFT (O(n log n)) dominates for very large numbers.

questions.q: How does the closest-pair-of-points algorithm use divide-and-conquer to achieve O(n log n) time?
options: It checks all pairs of points in O(n²) time, using no divide-and-conquer

It sorts points by x-coordinate, divides at the median x, recursively finds closest pairs in each half, then checks the strip of width 2d (d = current closest distance) around the dividing line; at most 6 points in the strip are close enough to be closer than d, limiting strip comparisons to O(n) per level → O(n log n) total
It uses a hash table to group nearby points
It requires points to be on a 2D grid with discrete coordinates
questions.explanation: Naive closest pair is O(n²) brute force. Divide-and-conquer: sort by x, divide at median x = X_mid, recursively find d_L and d_R. Let d = min(d_L, d_R). Points closer than d across the dividing line must lie in a strip [X_mid - d, X_mid + d]. In this strip, sort points by y-coordinate; any point has at most 5-7 points within distance d (by a geometric packing argument: a d×2d rectangle contains at most 6 non-overlapping d×(d/2) rectangles). So the strip comparison is O(n). Recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n log n) [or O(n) if pre-sorted y-coordinates are maintained] → T(n) = O(n log² n) or O(n log n). Space O(n).

questions.q: What is the substitution method for solving recurrences, and when is it practical?
options: Substitution method is only theoretical and never used in practice

Substitution method guesses a closed form T(n) = f(n), then proves it by induction showing T(n) ≤ f(n) (by inductive hypothesis on smaller inputs); practical when the form is obvious or conjectured from recursion tree analysis, but requires strong induction skills
Substitution method solves all recurrences automatically
Substitution is the same as the Master Theorem
questions.explanation: Substitution method: (1) guess form, e.g., T(n) = O(n log n); (2) substitute back into recurrence and simplify; (3) prove by induction. Example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n. Guess T(n) ≤ cn log n. Inductive step: assume T(n/2) ≤ c(n/2) log(n/2), then T(n) = 2·c(n/2) log(n/2) + n ≤ cn log(n/2) + n = cn(log n - 1) + n ≤ cn log n for c ≥ 1. Works when the guess is correct; pitfall is guessing wrong, leading to failed induction. Often used with recursion tree analysis to form an educated guess first.

questions.q: How does the recursion tree method provide intuition for solving recurrences?
options: It builds an actual tree data structure to represent the recursion

It draws a tree where each node represents a subproblem with its cost f(n), levels correspond to recursion depth, and the total cost is the sum of all nodes; useful for visualizing the pattern and guessing the closed form before applying substitution or Master Theorem
It only works for binary divide-and-conquer algorithms
It gives exact solutions without needing further proof
questions.explanation: For T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n, the tree has: (1) root with cost n; (2) two children each with cost n/2; (3) four grandchildren each with cost n/4; etc. At each level k (0 ≤ k ≤ log n), there are 2^k nodes with total cost 2^k · (n/2^k) = n. With log n levels, total cost = n · log n. This visually reveals the pattern without Master Theorem. For irregular recurrences (non-power subproblems, non-uniform costs), the tree method is invaluable. Once the pattern is evident from the tree, the induction (substitution method) is straightforward.

questions.q: How does binary search exemplify divide-and-conquer, and what property of the input does it require?
options: Binary search divides the array in half recursively, but requires a hash table for fast lookup

Binary search divides the sorted array in half, discarding the half that cannot contain the target, recursively searching the remaining half; requires the input to be sorted; T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → T(n) = O(log n), the fastest possible for comparison-based search
Binary search has the same time complexity as linear search
Binary search works on unsorted arrays if a random pivot is chosen
questions.explanation: Binary search is minimal divide-and-conquer: divide by comparing target to the middle element. If equal, done. If target is smaller, recurse on left half; else, recurse on right half. This ensures each recursive call reduces the problem size by half. Recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) → T(n) = O(log n). Requires sorted input: if unsorted, we cannot safely discard the half. For a sorted list of 1 million elements, binary search needs at most log₂(10⁶) ≈ 20 comparisons vs 500,000 for linear search. Enables efficient searching in databases, arrays, and BSTs.

questions.q: What is the time complexity of strassen's algorithm for matrix multiplication and how does it improve upon the standard O(n³) algorithm?
options: Strassen's algorithm is always slower than standard matrix multiplication

Standard algorithm: 8 recursive matrix multiplications of (n/2)×(n/2) blocks plus O(n²) addition → T(n) = 8T(n/2) + O(n²) = O(n³). Strassen's: 7 multiplications (via clever combinations) → T(n) = 7T(n/2) + O(n²) → T(n) = O(n^2.807), saving a factor of n^(3-2.807) ≈ n^0.193
Strassen's algorithm only works for 2×2 matrices
Both algorithms have the same asymptotic complexity; Strassen's has higher constant factors
questions.explanation: Standard algorithm multiplies two n×n matrices by computing each element as a dot product (n operations per element, n² elements) → O(n³). Strassen (1969) divides matrices into 4 blocks, requires 7 multiplications and 18 additions instead of 8 multiplications. Recurrence: T(n) = 7T(n/2) + Θ(n²) solves to T(n) = Θ(n^(log_2 7)) ≈ Θ(n^2.807). Faster for large n but has higher constant factors and numerical stability issues. Modern improvements (Coppersmith-Winograd, etc.) approach Θ(n^2.373). In practice, standard algorithm is preferred for moderate matrix sizes due to better cache locality.
